content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Thomas F. Schroeter 
City Attorney 
254 H Street 
Bakersfield, CA 93309 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-083 
Dear Mr. Schroeter: 
July 31, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tehachapi City Manager Greg 
Garrett regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act")1 and 
Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area oflaw, including Public Contract 
Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice 
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete 
and accurate. 
Regarding our advice on Section I 090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Kem County District 
Attorney's Office. (Section 1097.1 (c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. 
(Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that for purposes of Section 1090, the 
following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the 
requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does City Manager Garrett have a conflict of interest under the Act or Section 1090, if he 
accepts a position on the Community Governing Board ( .. Board") of Adventist Health, and there are 
future agreements or contracts between the hospital and the City involving hospital construction? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Under the Act, the City Manager does not have a reasonably foreseeable disqualifying 
interest in decisions related to the project. Additionally, under Section I 090 and Section I 091.5, the 
City Manager does not have a financial interest in any contracts between the City and the hospital 
under these facts. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Tille 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-083 
Page No. 2 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Tehachapi. You have been authorized by the City 
Manager to request this advice letter. He has been asked by Adventist Health, a faith-based, 
nonprofit health care corporation which is the owner and operator of the Tehachapi Hospital located 
within in the City, to sit on Board. 
Previously, as development of the hospital occurred, the City was active in addressing 
hospital zoning issues, right of way issues, and construction inspection. The hospital construction 
will soon be completed and the hospital operational. It is not anticipated that similar issues will 
arise in the near term. However, there could be a need for modifications to entitlements or new 
entitlements. There may also be agreements between the hospital and the City that could arise. 
The City Manager states that he will not be compensated for his Board duties and he cannot 
envision any decisions that he will make as City Manager involving the hospital. 
In the event the hospital seeks to expand or otherwise change its configuration, it must 
comply with the building and zoning requirements of the City Municipal Code, which are under the 
authority of the City Planning Commission and City Council. 
You wish to know whether the City Manager will have a conflict of interest under the Act or 
Section 1090, ifhe accepts a position on the Board and there are future agreements or contracts 
between the hospital and the City involving the recent hospital construction. 
ANALYSIS 
The Applicable Law 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. The Act's conflict of interest provisions only apply to conflicts 
of interests based on the following: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• Any real property.in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 


File No. A-17-083 
Page No. 3 
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• Any donor of, or any intennediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $4 70 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
• In addition, a public official's personal finances are deemed to be directly involved in a 
governmental decision that will have any financial effect on his or her personal finances or 
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.) 
Based on the facts you have provided, the City Manager has no financial interest in his 
Board position or in the hospital's contracts because the position is uncompensated and he receives 
no income from the Board or the hospital. Therefore, his position on the Board does not create a 
conflict of interest under the Act. 
Section I 090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies regardless of 
whether the tenns of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties.2 Section 1090 is concerned 
with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies.3 
Is the City Manager subject to Section 1090 and will he be making and participating in the 
making of a contract? 
Section 1090 provides, in part: "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are members." City 
managers are city officers or employees of a city and therefore are covered by this prohibition. 
Moreover, he is a public official based on his position on the Board of a community health care 
district. 
As a city employee and member of the health care district Board, the City Manager may 
make decisions to approve agreements between the community hospital and the City. Therefore, the 
decisions in question involve a contract. 
Does the City Manager have a financial interest in the contract? 
l 
Although Section 1090 nowhere specifically defines the tenn "financial interest," case law 
and Attorney General Opinions provide a definition that has been applied by various courts: 
2 Thomson l'. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646. 
1 Stigall l'. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569. 


"The phrase 'financially interested' as used in Government Code 
File No. A-17-083 
Page No. 4 
section I 090 means any financial interest which might interfere with a city 
officer's unqualified devotion to his public duty. The interest may be direct or 
indirect. It includes any monetary or proprietary benefit, or gain of any sort, or the 
contingent possibility of monetary or proprietary benefits. The interest is direct 
when the city officer, in his official capacity, does business with himself in his 
private capacity. The interest is indirect when the city officer, or the board of 
which he is a member, enters into a contract in his or its official capacity with an 
individual or business firm, which individual or business firm, by reason of the 
city officer's relationship to the individual or business firm at the time the 
contract is entered into, is in a position to render actual or potential pecuniary 
benefits directly or indirectly to the city officer based on the contract the 
individual or business firm has received. "4 
This definition has been applied by the courts in a variety of contexts.5 
For instance, under this formulation of "financial interest," it would be a Section I 090 
conflict of interest if the City Manager had a financial relationship with or interest in a potential 
hospital contractor to provide services under the agreement. 
Section I 091.S(a) states that an officer or employee shall not be deemed to be interested in a 
contract if his or her interest is any of the following: 
(8) That of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation, which, as 
one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the body or board or to which the 
body or board has a legal obligation to give particular consideration, and provided 
further that this interest is noted in its official records. 
For purposes of this paragraph, an officer is "noncompensated" even though he or she 
receives reimbursement from the nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation for necessary travel 
and other actual expenses incurred in performing the duties of his or her office. 
Under the facts you present, the City Manager does not have a financial interest in the City's 
contracts as contemplated by Section I 090 because he does not receive compensation for his Board 
position, receives no income from the hospital and has no financial relationship with or interest in a 
potential contracting entity. Thus, we conclude that Section I 090 does not apply in this context. 
4 People v. Watson (1971) 15 Cal.App.3d 28, 37-38. 
5 See People t', GnassJ2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1299, fn. 9; People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 
847,867; People,. Darb)•, 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 433-436; See also 89 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 258 (November 22, 2006). 


File No. A-17-083 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ECR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
Erne yn C. Rodriguez 
__ erfior Counsel, Legal Division

No. Under the Act, the City Manager does not have a reasonably foreseeable disqualifying 
interest in decisions related to the project. Additionally, under Section I 090 and Section I 091.5, the 
City Manager does not have a financial interest in any contracts between the City and the hospital 
under these facts.
